text: Laparoscopic esophagectomy and gastric pull-up for cancer or esophageal trauma have been reported as challenged but safe procedures that reproduce results of open surgery by several authors. A laparoscopic tailoring of a gastric tube with transviatal mediastinal dissection of the esophagus and gastric pull-up was performed in a young psychotic man referred for rupture of the cervical esophagus caused by a large foreign body's injection and clinical signs of mild mediastinitis.
The surgeon performs a left lateral cervicotomy to remove a foreign body, followed by a double cervical esophagostomy and drainage.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic esophagectomy and gastric pull-up for reconstruction, utilizing a four-part approach following the patient's recovery from mediastinitis.

The surgeon uses an ultrasonic dissector to divide the gastrocolic ligament, carefully preserving the gastroepiploic vessels. The short gastric vessels are then divided, completing the stomach mobilization.

The surgeon approaches the left gastric vessels from below the stomach, visualizing and dissecting the right and left cruciate. The abdominal portion of the esophagus is dissected free from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the left gastric vein between absorbable clips, ensuring hemostasis during the procedure.

The surgeon divides the left gastric artery between clips, carefully preserving the accessory hepatic artery arising from a common trunk. A gastric tube is fashioned using multiple applications of a linear stapler, with the first application through the right-sided cannula and subsequent applications through the left-sided cannula.

The surgeon widens the hiatus using the Pinotti technique and performs blunt and ultrasonic dissection of the esophagus up to the level of the azygos vein, following a plane along the anterior surface of the aorta. Care is taken to avoid opening the pleural sheath on both sides. Upward traction of the mediastinal structures is applied cautiously to prevent alteration of the cardiac rhythm. The dissection of the upper esophagus is completed from the neck.

The surgeon performs the gastric pull-up by applying gentle traction on the esophagus through the neck incision, guided by laparoscopic visualization. The length of the gastric tube is checked, and its tailoring is completed with two additional applications of a linear stapler.

A soft silicone drainage tube is inserted through the hiatus to drain the mediastinum. The esophagogastric anastomosis is performed using the Orringer technique, with the gastric tube's bottom end wrapped around the posterior aspect of the proximal esophageal stump. The axis opening is closed with interrupted absorbable sutures.

The post-op course was uneventful. X-ray contrast follow at post-op day nine showed good passage of contrast medium without leaks. Oral intake started right afterward. At six months and twelve months follow-up, the patient was completely recovered following a regular oral diet.
Whatever the approach to mediastinal dissection of the esophagus, the laparoscopic tailoring of the stomach tube is a safe and advisable procedure for both cancer and trauma patients.
